steps.title: Read histogram frequencies and locate median positions
steps.content:
From the histogram, the original 11 years distribute as: 2 in [40, 45), 3 in [45, 50), 3 in [50, 55), 1 in [55, 60), and 2 in [75, 80).

The original median is the 6th value (middle of 11 ordered values).

Cumulative count: positions 1–2 in [40, 45), positions 3–5 in [45, 50), positions 6–8 in [50, 55), position 9 in [55, 60), positions 10–11 in [75, 80). The original median is the 6th value, located in [50, 55).

steps.title: Statement I: Does removing 76.2°F decrease the mean?
steps.content:
For the mean to decrease when a value is removed, that value must be greater than the current mean.

The data clusters in [40, 60), with only 2 values in [75, 80).

The mean is approximately $\frac{2(42.5) + 3(47.5) + 3(52.5) + 1(57.5) + 2(76.5)}{11} \approx \frac{595}{11} \approx 54°F$.

Since 76.2°F $\gg$ 54°F, removing it makes the numerator smaller while the denominator shrinks from 11 to 10, thus the new mean $= \frac{\text{old sum} - 76.2}{10} <$ original mean. Statement I is TRUE.

steps.title: Statement II: Does removing 76.2°F decrease the median?
steps.content:
Removing one value from [75, 80) leaves 10 values. The new median is the average of the 5th and 6th values. Both the 5th and 6th values can fall in the same neighborhood as the original 6th value (the median bin), so the new median is not guaranteed to be strictly less than the original median — it depends on the exact integer values, which the histogram does not pin down.

Statement II is therefore not necessarily true.

steps.title: Both statements are true
steps.content: Statement I must be true (removing a value far above the mean lowers the mean), but Statement II is not guaranteed by the histogram alone. Only Statement I must be true. That matches choice A — A is correct.
